subject: reading
title: Grade 3 Reading — Middle of Year (MOY) Test 2
instructions: Read each question carefully. Choose the best answer.

passages.title: The Lost Library Book
passages.text:
Maya loved Fridays because that was library day at Pine Street School. Every Friday, Mr. Lopez, the librarian, let each student pick one book to take home for the week. Last Friday, Maya chose a thick book about ocean animals. The cover showed a glowing jellyfish, and she could not wait to read it.

But on Monday morning, the book was gone. Maya looked under her bed, behind the couch, and inside her backpack three times. Her heart sank like a stone. The book had to be returned that very day, or she would owe a fee. Worse than the fee, she did not want to disappoint Mr. Lopez, who had trusted her.

"Did you check the car?" asked her older brother, Theo, as he tied his shoes. Maya had not. She raced outside and flung open the car door. There it was, wedged between the seats, where it had slipped on the ride home. She let out a long breath of relief.

That afternoon, Maya handed the book to Mr. Lopez right on time. "I almost lost it," she admitted, her cheeks warm. Mr. Lopez smiled gently. "Everyone misplaces things sometimes. The important thing is that you kept looking instead of giving up." Maya nodded. From then on, she always put her library books in the same spot on her desk, so they would never wander off again.

passages.title: The Crow and the Pitcher
passages.text:
Long ago, on a hot and dusty day, a thirsty crow flew over the dry land looking for water. The sun beat down, and the crow's throat felt as rough as sand. For hours she found nothing but cracked earth and empty streams.

At last, she spotted a tall clay pitcher resting beside an old barn. The crow swooped down, hoping for a cool drink. But when she peered inside, she saw that the water was very low. No matter how she stretched her neck, her beak could not reach the water at the bottom.

The crow did not fly away in anger. Instead, she stopped to think. Nearby lay a pile of small, smooth pebbles. One by one, the crow picked up a pebble in her beak and dropped it into the pitcher. With each pebble, the water rose a little higher, climbing slowly toward the top.

The crow kept working, even though her wings grew tired. At last, the water rose high enough for her to drink. She tipped her beak in and drank deeply, feeling cool and refreshed. As she flew away, the crow had learned that a clever mind and steady effort can solve a problem that strength alone cannot.

passages.title: How Bees Make Honey
passages.text:
Honey is one of nature's sweetest treats, but it does not appear by magic. It takes thousands of busy honeybees working together to make even a single jar. The process begins in fields full of flowers.

First, worker bees fly from flower to flower collecting a sweet liquid called nectar. A bee sips the nectar with its long, straw-like tongue and stores it in a special pouch inside its body called the honey stomach. One bee may visit more than a hundred flowers before flying back to the hive.

Next, the bee returns home and passes the nectar to other bees in its mouth. These bees chew the nectar for a while, adding special juices that begin to change it. Then they spread the nectar inside small wax cells in the honeycomb. The nectar is still very watery at this stage, so the bees must dry it out.

To dry the nectar, the bees fan it with their wings. The moving air makes the extra water disappear, and the nectar slowly thickens into honey. Finally, the bees seal each cell with a cap of wax to keep the honey fresh. Bees store this honey as food for the cold winter, when no flowers bloom. Luckily for us, a healthy hive often makes more honey than the bees need, so beekeepers can gather the extra to share.

questions.questionText: Why does Maya love Fridays?
options: It is the last day of school
It is library day at her school
She gets to ride in the car
questions.explanation: The passage says Maya loved Fridays because that was library day at Pine Street School.

questions.questionText: What kind of book did Maya choose to take home?
questions.explanation: Maya chose a thick book about ocean animals with a glowing jellyfish on the cover.

questions.questionText: What does the phrase 'Her heart sank like a stone' show about how Maya felt?
questions.explanation: A heart sinking like a stone is a way of showing that Maya felt sad and worried when the book was gone.

questions.questionText: Where did Maya finally find the missing book?
questions.explanation: Maya found the book wedged between the seats of the car, where it had slipped on the ride home.

questions.questionText: Why was Maya more worried about disappointing Mr. Lopez than about paying a fee?
options: Mr. Lopez had trusted her with the book
She did not have any money
questions.explanation: The passage says that worse than the fee, she did not want to disappoint Mr. Lopez, who had trusted her.

questions.questionText: In the sentence 'She let out a long breath of relief,' the word 'relief' means a feeling of —
questions.explanation: Maya felt relief, meaning her worry went away, once she found the book.

questions.questionText: What lesson does Maya learn by the end of the story?
options: Keeping things in one place helps you not lose them
Cars are good places to store books
questions.explanation: At the end, Maya always puts her library books in the same spot so they will not wander off again.

questions.questionText: How does Theo help Maya in the story?
options: He finds the book for her
He suggests she check the car
questions.explanation: Theo asks 'Did you check the car?', which gives Maya the idea that leads her to the book.

questions.questionText: Which TWO places did Maya look before finding the book? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: The passage says Maya looked under her bed, behind the couch, and inside her backpack.

questions.questionText: The author writes that Maya looked inside her backpack 'three times.' This detail mostly helps the reader understand that Maya —
options: was searching very carefully and was nervous
did not really want the book
questions.explanation: Looking three times shows that Maya searched carefully because she was worried about the missing book.

questions.questionText: Why was the crow flying over the land at the beginning of the story?
options: She was looking for her nest
questions.explanation: The thirsty crow flew over the dry land looking for water.

questions.questionText: What problem did the crow have when she found the pitcher?
options: The water was too low to reach
questions.explanation: The water was very low, and the crow's beak could not reach it at the bottom.

questions.questionText: How did the crow get the water to rise?
options: She dropped pebbles into the pitcher
questions.explanation: The crow dropped small pebbles into the pitcher one by one, and the water rose higher with each one.

questions.questionText: In the sentence 'her throat felt as rough as sand,' the author compares the crow's throat to sand to show that the crow was —
questions.explanation: Comparing the throat to rough, dry sand shows how thirsty the crow was.

questions.questionText: What is the lesson, or moral, of this fable?
options: It is best to fly away from problems
A clever mind and steady effort can solve problems
questions.explanation: The ending states that a clever mind and steady effort can solve a problem that strength alone cannot.

questions.questionText: When the crow could not reach the water, she 'did not fly away in anger. Instead, she stopped to think.' This shows the crow was —
questions.explanation: Stopping to think instead of giving up in anger shows the crow was patient and smart.

questions.questionText: Why does the author mention that 'her wings grew tired'?
options: To show the crow gave up
To show the crow worked hard and did not quit
To show the crow was hurt
To show the crow flew away
questions.explanation: Even with tired wings the crow kept working, which shows she did not quit until the job was done.

questions.questionText: How did the crow feel after she finally drank the water?
questions.explanation: After drinking deeply, the crow felt cool and refreshed.

questions.questionText: What is the main topic of this article?
questions.explanation: The article explains the steps bees use to make honey.

questions.questionText: What is the sweet liquid called that bees collect from flowers?
questions.explanation: Worker bees collect a sweet liquid called nectar from flowers.

questions.questionText: The author uses words like 'First,' 'Next,' and 'Finally.' These words help the reader understand —
questions.explanation: Sequence words like First, Next, and Finally show the order of the steps bees follow.

questions.questionText: In the article, the 'honey stomach' is best described as —
options: a special pouch where a bee stores nectar
a wax cap on the honeycomb
questions.explanation: The passage says the honey stomach is a special pouch inside the bee's body where it stores nectar.

questions.questionText: How do bees dry the watery nectar to turn it into honey?
options: They place it in the sun
They fan it with their wings
questions.explanation: Bees fan the nectar with their wings so the extra water disappears and it thickens into honey.

questions.questionText: Why do bees store honey in the honeycomb?
options: To use as food during the cold winter
questions.explanation: Bees store honey as food for the cold winter, when no flowers bloom.

questions.questionText: Why can beekeepers gather honey without harming a healthy hive?
options: A healthy hive often makes more honey than the bees need
Bees make new honey every hour
questions.explanation: The passage explains a healthy hive often makes more honey than the bees need, so the extra can be gathered.

questions.questionText: According to the article, one bee may visit MORE THAN how many flowers before flying back to the hive? Write the number.
questions.explanation: The passage states one bee may visit more than a hundred flowers before returning to the hive.

questions.questionText: Which TWO things do bees do AFTER they bring nectar back to the hive? Select all that apply.
options: Chew the nectar and add special juices
Fly out to find a hundred flowers
Seal the cells with a cap of wax
Sip nectar with a straw-like tongue
questions.explanation: After returning, bees chew the nectar adding juices, and later seal the cells with wax. Sipping nectar and finding flowers happen before returning to the hive.

questions.questionText: The author begins by saying honey 'does not appear by magic.' This sentence mostly helps the reader understand that —
options: honey is a kind of magic trick
making honey takes real work by many bees
bees do not really make honey
questions.explanation: Saying it does not appear by magic points out that making honey takes hard work from thousands of bees.
